card.title: The Exposure Triangle
card.teaser: Three dials. Endless photos. Master the triangle and your camera stops being a mystery box.
card.coverImagePrompt: A vintage film camera on a wooden desk with a glowing aperture iris, three dials labeled ISO, shutter, aperture softly lit from above, cinematic product shot

lessons.title: Why is my photo too dark?
lessons.body: You point your camera at a sunset, press the shutter, and get a muddy black rectangle. Point it at a snowy field and everything glows like a nuclear blast. Your eyes had no problem. Your camera is confused. The reason is a beautiful little negotiation called the exposure triangle — and once you see it, you see it everywhere.

visual.imagePrompt: A split-screen photo: left side a pitch-black sunset, right side a blown-out snow scene, dramatic comparison

lessons.title: Three sliders, one bucket
lessons.body: Imagine filling a bucket with light. You have three taps: aperture (how wide the opening is), shutter speed (how long the tap runs), and ISO (how sensitive the bucket is to what it catches). A 'correct' exposure is just the right amount of water. Changing one tap means adjusting the others — which is where the art begins.

keyTerms.definition: The size of the lens opening, measured in f-stops like f/2.8 or f/16
How long the sensor is exposed to light, like 1/500 s or 1 s
The sensor's sensitivity to light — higher ISO brightens dark scenes but adds noise

visual.imagePrompt: An elegant triangle diagram with aperture, shutter, and ISO at each corner, each icon gently glowing

lessons.title: Each dial has a side effect
lessons.body: The triangle isn't just about brightness — each dial trades one thing for another. A wide aperture (small f-number) brightens the photo but blurs the background. A slow shutter lets in more light but turns moving subjects into streaks. A high ISO salvages low light but sprinkles noisy grain across your image. Every photo you love made a choice about which trade-off mattered most.

visual.imagePrompt: Three horizontal sliders labeled aperture, shutter, ISO with visual examples of each effect floating beside them

lessons.title: The same scene, three photos
lessons.body: Imagine a coffee cup on a café table by a window. With a wide f/1.8, the cup is crisp and the window behind it melts into dreamy blur. Switch to f/16 and suddenly the entire café is in focus. Same scene, same light — just one dial. Pro photographers don't see camera settings; they see the emotional decision each dial makes.
lessons.funFact: An f-stop is actually a ratio: f/2 means the aperture diameter equals the focal length divided by 2. That's why the numbers look weird — they're fractions in disguise.

visual.imagePrompt: Three photos of the same coffee cup by a window, each with different depth of field, side by side

lessons.title: You are the triangle
lessons.body: Your camera's auto mode is just a committee deciding the triangle for you. Learning manual mode isn't about being a snob — it's about the moment you realize every image is a choice, and you start making it. Once the triangle clicks, you don't think about exposure anymore. You just take the picture you meant to take.

quiz.question: Which setting controls how long light hits the sensor?
quiz.explanation: Shutter speed controls the duration of light exposure — shorter freezes motion, longer captures it as blur.

quiz.question: What is a common side effect of using a very high ISO?
quiz.explanation: Raising ISO amplifies the signal from the sensor — and amplifies its noise, giving photos a grainy look.

quiz.question: Which aperture gives you the blurriest background?
quiz.explanation: A smaller f-number means a wider opening, shallower depth of field, and a creamier background blur.
